Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to blackrock.com for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 4% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.43%; for Investor A shares: 0.68%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 2.75% (Institutional shares) and 2.56% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • Structured products and emerging market debt contributed to performance. U.S. rates, U.S. investment grade credit, and U.S. agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) detracted.
  • We reduced top-line duration (sensitivity to interest rate movements) from an overweight position to a modest underweight. We preferred the front-end of the yield curve and held a steepening bias. The fund’s U.S. investment grade credit exposure was reduced and the agency MBS position was increased to overweight. In addition, we favored high- quality securitized exposure.

Contributors

Detractors

The allocation to securitized assets, specifically commercial mortgage-backed securities, was the largest contributor, as high-quality securitized spreads largely tightened for most of January and February. Emerging market positioning, particularly Mexican and Brazilian local rates, was beneficial as tariff concerns declined in January before reaccelerating toward quarter-end.

The U.S. rates position detracted from performance. As rates rallied in late January and into February, we held a steepening bias and reduced the overweight exposure, given our view that parts of the yield curve were overbought. Throughout February, the yield curve flattened and rates rallied, which caused the fund’s underweight position in the “belly” of the curve to detract. Positions in U.S. investment grade credit and agency MBS weighed on returns due to policy uncertainty and heightened rate volatility.

Further insight

During the quarter, the fund’s overweight duration exposure was reduced to a modest underweight position (5.84 years versus the benchmark’s 5.85 years). This was done largely by shifting from an overweight position in the 7-10-year part of the yield curve to an underweight exposure. We held a steepening bias in the belief that the long-end of the curve might sell off due to pressure on term premium expansion as a result of fiscal deficit concerns. We continued to prefer the front-end of the curve as yields appeared attractive. In spread sectors, we tactically rotated across sectors as we continued to look for attractive, high-quality sources of income.

Federal National Mortgage 12.48
Uniform Mbs 9.25

Morgan Stanley (MS) 1.14

Vici Properties Lp (VICI) 0.96
Goldman Sachs Group Inc/The (GS) 0.83

Investment approach

Invests at least 80% of its assets in bonds and maintains an average portfolio duration that is within ±20% of the duration of the benchmark. The fund may invest up to 25% of its assets in foreign issuers, of which 10% may be invested in emerging markets issuers. Up to 10% of the fund’s assets may be exposed to non-U.S. currency risk.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Bond values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. Fixed income risks include interest-rate and credit risk. Typically, when interest rates rise, there is a corresponding decline in bond values. Credit risk refers to the possibility that the bond issuer will not be able to make principal and interest payments. Principal of mortgage- or asset- backed securities normally may be prepaid at any time, reducing the yield and market value of those securities. Obligations of U.S. gov't agencies are supported by varying degrees of credit but generally are not backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. gov't. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility. International investing involves special risks including, but not limited to political risks, currency fluctuations, illiquidity and volatility. These risks may be heightened for investments in emerging markets.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management team as of March 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

data.title: VIOV: Criteria Too Narrow In Scope; Better Options Elsewhere
data.description:
What is VIOV?

The Vanguard S&P Small-Cap 600 Value Index Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:VIOV) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as ETF) that invests in small-cap American listed stocks. The point of this ETF is to have a cheap, passively managed option to track outperforming stocks within the S&P 600 Small-Cap Value Index, with the fund manager rebalancing and reviewing the fund quarterly or less for investment opportunities.

VIOV's benchmark target is the S&P 600 Small-Cap Value Index, and this article will tell you whether it's worth investing with VIOV or another asset manager/fund when thinking about investing in small-cap equities. The underlying benchmark generally includes U.S. small-capitalization companies that have value qualities to allow investors to try to outperform the larger small-cap bucket, notably filtering on lower P/B, P/E and P/S ratios. Meanwhile, passive investing ETF's mean that the fund managers don't make many trades, and only rebalance every once in a while, versus active managers, who have the mandate to buy and sell stocks more often if they feel that there's a compelling investment opportunity.

After further review, my analysis shows that VIOV is not worth an investment vs. other passively managed peers. The fund has underperformed other small-cap and small-cap value ETF's over the past few years, and I find the criteria as simplistic among such a diverse economic small-cap set to truly warrant outperformance. I recommend investors buy the Vanguard Small-Cap Value ETF (VBR) if investors want passively managed, low fee options small-cap value options with higher returns, or simply go with Vanguard Small-Cap ETF (VB) if you want a blended small-cap ETF that has outperformed VIOV.

Fund Breakdown

VIOV was launched on September 7, 2010, by Vanguard with a mandate to track the performance of a benchmark index that measures the investment return of small-capitalization value stocks in the United States. The mandate makes sense, but I'm a bit dubious of these passively managed funds that try to find "growth" or "value" within these already narrow distinct buckets (i.e. 600 small-cap stocks). If you look at the returns on many of these funds, VIOV included, there isn't always a link to see the risk vs. reward returns that are mentioned in the prospectus strategy as the target. Additionally, while the fund is tagged as passively managed, the portfolio turnover was 52%, which means that the portfolio managers were trading pretty often (most passively managed funds are below 30% turnover). I'm already skeptical of managers trying to outperform the market, so between the active tendencies and simplistic filter criteria, I was already a bit skeptical of the fund.

That aside, the fund has been trading for years, with a NAV of ~$1.2Bn and a daily volume of over 90,000 shares traded. But upon further review of the ETF's results, I'm skeptical that the fund has fully achieved what it set out to do. Below is a chart over the past 1 and 2 year timeframes showing returns slightly below other passively managed peers with similar low fee structures. Additionally, the fund facts noted that the ETF underperformed its benchmark since inception.

The fund employs a 0.10% yearly ETF expense ratio for fees to the asset manager, similar and negligible to other passively managed peers like VB and VBR. The other small-cap value fund, VBR, tracks the CRSP US Small Cap Value Index, which is a more fulsome representation of 850+ small caps vs. S&P's methodology of 600, which is then ultimately reduced by the filter criteria. While VIOV is decent, but I don't think it's set up as an outperformer based on its previous results and history.

Previous Results & Fund Portfolio

When comparing VIOV's portfolio to peers, there are subtle differences in the portfolio breakdown, but not many. The portfolio resembles the index benchmark, so if you are an investor who tends to like S&P's methodologies to find outperforming value stocks, then have at it. Overall, though, this fund is the same as the benchmark, which has been an underperformer.

From a risk perspective, a beta of 1.22 notes that the fund will have more volatility than the benchmark - and that has borne out in past results with lower lows and some, but rarely long-lasting, bounce backs. Additionally, when evaluating VIOV's risk-adjusted returns, I analyzed the Sharpe ratio to see whether the fund has generated excess returns over the risk-free rate when adjusted for total volatility. Unfortunately, VIOV's Sharpe ratio for the fund is -0.06, well below the score of 1+ to prove strong risk-adjusted returns. And from a macroeconomic perspective, small-cap growth stocks are more sensitive to interest rate fluctuations. With the Federal Reserve taking a wait and see approach to rate cuts given the fluctuating tariff policies, small-cap stocks may be left in limbo, after the last few years of underperformance due to higher borrowing costs. Given VIOV's underperformance vs. peers, I'd rather buy VB or VBR if I expect a rally in small-caps to occur.

Risks & Bottom Line

The bottom line for this fund, in my opinion, is that the juice is not worth the squeeze. There's simply not enough good news to justify this "value" small cap fund, and investors can simply get normal small cap exposure through VB or VBR. VIOV doesn't have enough valuable differentiation from its filtered criteria and hasn't outperformed to convince me it's a better ETF alternative than other passive counterparts. While there are risks of small-caps being hit by tariffs and having more volatility than the broader index, I don't think VIOV is the best option of the bunch.

data.title: From Selloff To Setup - Enphase Reloads
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Enphase (NASDAQ:ENPH) is turning a geopolitical headwind into a strategic tailwind. By relocating 85% of its microinverter production to the U.S., the company is cushioning itself against fluctuating tariffs while unlocking key tax credits. With expanding domestic capacity, innovative product rollouts, and broader global reach, Enphase is positioning itself for resilient growth, even if trade tensions escalate again. Meanwhile, new products and Served Available Market (SAM) expansion in Europe and EMs support growth, despite margin pressures from Chinese cell pack tariffs.

Enphase's U.S. production pivot reduces tariff risk and enhances tax credit eligibility, thereby stabilizing margins. New products and global expansion aim to drive topline growth. But reliance on Chinese battery cells, soft U.S. demand, and a high forward P/E signal near-term pressure. Despite a steep selloff of around 60% over the past year and 70% over three years, investors may want to stay on the sidelines until margin pressures ease and demand shows clearer signs of recovery.

SAM Expansion and Microinverter Production Shift in the US Amid Tariffs Drama

Enphase is moving towards a diversified supply base for microinverters to mitigate the risks associated with global trade disruptions, including Trump tariffs. Enphase has shifted 85% of its global microinverter production to the US, with a quarterly capacity of 5 million units. This diversification considerably reduces exposure to the 145% (now reduced) tariffs on Chinese imports and the 10% tariffs on imports from other countries.

The tariffs may now have "minimal impact" on microinverters and accessories for Q2. There is a temporary reduction of the US tariffs on Chinese goods to 30% for 90 days. However, Enphase's limited dependence on Chinese manufacturing for microinverters ensures bottom-line stability if 145% tariffs resume.

Moreover, the company has a focus on advancements in batteries and microinverters to push the topline up. For instance, the upcoming 4th-gen IQ Battery 10C is expected to ramp up production in Q2 of FY25 and integrate battery management and power conversion. This can reduce wall space by 60% along with simplification of installation with components like the IQ Combiner 6C and Meter Collar. This system lowers total system costs for backup applications. Additionally, the IQ9 microinverter leverages gallium nitride (GAN) technology, which yields a 10% higher power output at a similar cost structure. So, it reduces the cost per watt and targets the commercial solar market with 480-volt 3-phase systems.

These advancements can enhance Enphase's product appeal and potentially capture a larger share of the residential and commercial markets. The IQ Balcony Solar product, released in Germany and Belgium, is expandable to other regions. The product extends the addressable market by 400 megawatts annually in Germany. This type of product may continue to support topline growth by addressing core requirements in the energy market and expanding the addressable market.

Enphase's current domestic manufacturing for batteries boosts its product capability to qualify for US tax incentives by matching domestic content requirements. In Q1-FY25, Enphase shipped 44 megawatt hours of IQ Batteries from its Texas facility. The facility uses domestically produced microinverters, battery management systems, and packaging, despite sourcing cell packs from China. The company's capability to produce 1.21 million microinverters domestically in Q1-FY25 and qualify for 45x production tax credits. This factor has improved its gross margin to 48.9% (non-GAAP) in Q1, which includes a $37.9 million net Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) benefit.

However, tariffs on Chinese cell packs may reduce gross margins by 6-8% in Q3. Enphase is actively countering this through non-Chinese cell sources. There are plans to fully offset the impact by Q2-26. The domestic manufacturing strength, along with supply base adjustments, may derive bottom-line growth in the US market.

Internationally, strategic market expansion in Europe and EMs diversifies Enphase's topline into high-growth markets. In Q1-FY25, revenue from Europe jumped by 7% QoQ based on the new FlexPhase battery in Germany amid a 9% decline in sell-through due to seasonality. The company is expanding its product portfolio across Europe with the IQ EV charger, backup-capable batteries, and the Balcony Solar product and targeting underpenetrated markets (like Italy, Spain, and Poland).

In Germany, the FlexPhase battery's 30% backup attach rate indicates strong demand. Whereas the Netherlands' shift to solar-plus-battery products is in line with Enphase's current capabilities. Moreover, EMs like Puerto Rico, India, and Brazil can also support topline growth. There is a rising demand for high domestic content batteries in Puerto Rico and growing sales of IQ8P microinverters in India and Brazil. In short, these expansion points have increased Enphase's SAM to over $25 billion and reduced dependence on the US market, struggling with higher interest and policy uncertainty.

Gross Margin Compression by Q3 FY25 Amid China Tariff and Weak Demand

A primary issue for Enphase's bottom line is its dependence on Chinese-sourced battery cell packs. This exposes the company to tariff-related cost pressures, as 95% of lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cells globally are produced in China. Enphase currently sources its battery cell packs from Chinese suppliers. So, the resumable 125% tariff on exports to the US (temporarily reduced to 10% for 90 days) by China may reduce Enphase's gross margin once the pre-tariff inventory is depleted. This margin compression can be a big issue as Enphase plans to absorb the majority of the cost increase rather than fully passing it to customers.

Therefore, This decision is based on the expectation that non-Chinese cell sources may be qualified by Q2-FY26. However, the qualification process requires heavy testing and any delays can extend the margin impact. Further, there is a competitive run for non-Chinese LFP supplies. Enphase's competitors face similar tariff pressures that can increase costs due to limited availability. This can hit Enphase's capability to maintain profitability and current pricing in its battery segment.

Domestically, Enphase's sensitivity to macro and policy uncertainties led to a 13% QoQ decline in US revenue in Q1-FY25. There were other issues like seasonality and softening demand led by high-interest rates and the financial issues of a large national lease provider. High-interest rates are affecting 30% of residential solar transactions that are still financed through loans. This reduces affordability and hits hard on demand due to uncertainty surrounding the IRA. The potential repeal of the 30% investment tax credit (ITC) in FY26 creates a demand stagnation among interested customers. These policy issues are delaying installations and impacting sell-through (down 18% QoQ in Q1-FY25). Any further unfavorable policy changes can suppress demand more and can hit hard on projected topline growth in its largest market (the US), which accounts for 74% of Q1-FY25 revenue.

Regional market issues in Europe (like in France) can also cap the international topline growth. Revenue from Europe increased in Q1-FY25 due to new product introductions, but the overall business environment is still problematic with a 9% decline in sell-through. For instance, in France, recent utility rate cuts have slowed the market demand. Enphase expects demand to remain muted for the next two quarters until a lower VAT takes effect in October this year. This temporary demand suppression in France limits near-term revenue potential.

Similarly, the Netherlands' softening demand is due to winter seasonality. Here, the anticipated phase-out of net metering by FY27 requires a market transition to solar-plus-storage products that can be a cost burden on Enphase's resources and delay revenue growth in this market. Overall, these issues can continue to negatively impact market sentiment for the stock.

Valuation Check: is Enphase a Value Play or a Value Trap?

On the surface, Enphase's valuation may appear appealing. It has a trailing P/E ratio of 17.76, which is lower than the sector average, and a PEG ratio of 0.65, indicating possible undervaluation in relation to growth. But look deeper, and warning signs emerge. A forward P/E of close to 60, more than double the sector median, indicates a steep fall in the bottom line in the near term. High EV/EBIT and Price/Book multiples, which far exceed the sector average, suggest that investors may be paying too high a price for lower future returns.

Although the firm enjoys a history of robust growth, recent soft demand trends and tariff pressures threaten margin compression. Unless profitability rebounds quickly, current multiples may not withstand the test of time. Therefore, although ENPH is not overpriced per se, the disconnect between compressed margins and still-rich GAAP valuations suggests a potential value trap if the favorable narrative for tax credits and U.S. manufacturing does not benefit the firm with sustained earnings growth.

Takeaway

Enphase is actively de-risking its supply chain and expanding globally, with innovation and U.S. manufacturing driving tax benefits and topline potential. But margin pressures from Chinese battery tariffs and soft demand remain key hurdles. It's a high-risk, high-reward bet, better suited for patient investors with a long-term view.

Expenses for Class I shares: Total 0.81%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses (dividend expense, interest expense, acquired fund fees and expenses and certain other fund expenses) 0.67%; Net, Excluding Investment Related Expenses 0.67%.

Refer to (800)-441-7762 for most recent month-end performance.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted a return of -5.47% (Class I shares) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • The fund’s underperformance of its benchmark was led by trending sentiment insights and macro-thematic measures, which struggled amid the changing leadership dynamics.
  • From a sector-positioning perspective, the fund remained largely neutral. However, there were slight overweight allocations to the health care and consumer staples sectors, and slight underweight exposures to the industrials and utilities sectors.
Contributors Detractors
Certain insights provided support during the quarter. Fundamental quality measures looking at balance sheet strength and evaluating expected company default frequency helped to correctly position the fund in the health care sector. Elsewhere, sentiment insights using alternative data to capture retail preferences and credit card transactions worked well. Looking at informed investor positioning and using text-based measures to gauge broker sentiment also helped offset losses. These insights overcame sharp de- risking in early March as investors rotated out of previous momentum winners. The fund’s weak relative performance was mainly driven by macro-thematic insights. Measures looking at historical data to identify similarities in the current economic regime incorrectly positioned the fund against changing leadership dynamics. Other macro-thematic insights evaluating job-hiring trends proved wrong-footed. Collectively, these helped to motivate unsuccessful overweight positioning in the information technology sector. This coincided with weakness driven by fundamental quality insights looking at cash-flow generation and long-term asset efficiency. These measures struggled during earnings season.

Further insight

After touching all-time highs in February, global equities declined sharply to end in negative territory. U.S. market stress drove the sell-off as uncertainty about the new administration’s policy agenda simultaneously stoked recession concerns and market volatility. Combined with cooling sentiment toward the artificial intelligence (AI) opportunity, after Chinese companies released rival, low-cost AI models, market leadership rotated as elevated U.S. valuations came under pressure. U.S. small-cap stocks continued to underperform large-cap equities during the quarter. Unsurprisingly, despite concerns about a tariff-led acceleration of inflation, economic growth concerns drove a sharp drop in bond yields. In contrast, international markets proved resilient as investors repositioned for the end of the U.S. exceptionalism theme.

Investment approach

Invests at least 80% of its net assets in large cap equity securities, and derivatives that have similar economic characteristics to such securities, that are components of, or have characteristics similar to, the securities included in the Russell 1000® Index.

data.title: Canadian Apartment Properties: Stable Fundamentals But Lacking Clear Upside Catalysts
data.description:
All figures are listed in $CAD unless otherwise noted.

All financial data is from Seeking Alpha unless otherwise noted.

Thesis

Canadian Apartment Properties REIT (TSX:CAR.UN:CA)(OTC:CDPYF), referred to as "CAR" herein, recently reported its results for the first quarter of 2025. While we believe Canadian residential REITs are generally undervalued and would rate most as a "Buy," real estate investments should be evaluated relative to their peers, given the similar risk profiles across the sector. In CAR's case, mixed Q1 results, a relative valuation that doesn't stand out, and a near-term outlook with more headwinds than tailwinds lead us to initiate coverage with a "Hold" rating.

First Quarter in Review

We view CAR's performance in the first quarter of 2025 as mixed. On the positive side, occupancy remains strong despite continued rent increases. However, we see some challenges this quarter, particularly around NOI margin pressure and the decline in renewal and turnover lease rates.

Occupancy came in at 97.6%, slightly down from 98.0% in 2024. If we exclude the European portfolio, which is in the process of being sold, occupancy was 97.8% compared to 97.9% last year. Quarterly, occupancy actually rose by 0.4%, which we see as a positive given the usual winter slowdown. And although occupancy is down 0.4% year over year, a roughly 26% increase in average monthly rent has helped offset the impact.

The NOI margin dropped to 62.4% in Q1 2025, down from 64.2% in the same quarter last year and 65.7% for full-year 2024. We think this was mostly due to higher than expected utility costs, likely driven by a colder winter, which increased 1.7% year over year. Meanwhile, operating revenues fell by 8.2%. Property taxes and other expenses also contributed, as they only declined by 0.1% and 7.6% respectively, meaning the drop in revenues outpaced the reduction in costs. We expect some relief on this front in the coming quarters, particularly in utilities, now that the carbon tax has been removed on certain fuels starting April 1, 2025.

As expected, rent growth has slowed. The weighted average monthly rent increase was 3.4%, with suite turnovers at 7.2% and renewals at 2.9%. That compares to 4.1% last year, with much stronger suite turnover growth at 23.2% and renewals at 3.0%. Only two years ago, suite turnovers were sitting at nearly 30% rent increases, so this is a big shift.

Overall, CAR is holding steady. They're continuing to deleverage and upgrade the quality of their portfolio. That said, we see both catalysts and risks ahead, which we outline below.

Relative Valuation

We believe the most relevant valuation metric for REITs is the price-to-book ratio since investment properties and their associated liabilities (i.e. mortgages) make up the bulk of a REIT's book value.

Over the past few years, immigration and limited new supply have been the dominant themes in the residential market, but the market is shifting. Rent growth on turnover units is slowing, suggesting a tightening rental market. Renewal rent increases remain more stable, though they're generally capped by rent control (Ontario and BC for this context).

With sentiment starting to turn and the growth narrative fading, we expect CAR's three-year average P/B to remain slightly above the current level. As shown in the figure below, the current P/B sits at 0.75x, just below the historical average of 0.81x. If CAR were to return to its historical average valuation, the implied share price would be approximately $44.99, which is about 8% above the current level.

Although this metric suggests some potential upside, it's not sufficient to support a strong conviction that CAR is undervalued.

However, one common critique we have for REITs is that the IFRS fair value of investment properties (reflected as the carrying value on the balance sheet) often appears inflated to us. That's why we were encouraged to hear CAR's management acknowledge this concern. They stated:

"We are securing prices that are at, or above, previously reported IFRS fair values at the time of negotiation, further substantiating our robust valuations process and conservative Net Asset Value (NAV)."

This could suggest that the current IFRS values may understate the true market value of CAR's properties, meaning the current P/B ratio might be lower than it should be. However, the differences in actuality aren't material enough to fully justify this claim.

Looking at CAR relative to peers, we think it's fairly valued. Historically, CAR has traded at a slight premium to Boardwalk REIT (BEI.UN:CA) and a modest discount to Killam REIT (KMP.UN:CA). On average, CAR's P/B multiple has been about 1.01 times Boardwalk's and 0.98 times Killam's, which aligns with where it trades today (less so for Killam, but the margin is still close).

With CAR's current P/B multiple sitting at 1.02 times Boardwalk's and 1.08 times Killam's, we don't see a significant pricing gap that would support a strong view either way. This adds to our belief that CAR is fairly valued at current levels.

Much like our take on the Q1 results, any upside suggested by CAR's valuation relative to its historical average is offset by the downside when compared to its peers.

Debt Levels Are Good, But Offer No Clear Advantage

It could be argued that CAR deserves a slight premium over both Boardwalk and Killam, given its stronger balance sheet. CAR has the lowest debt-to-gross book value ratio, the highest interest coverage ratio, and the lowest weighted average interest rate among the three, at 3.16% compared to 3.21% for Boardwalk and 3.48% for Killam. These metrics point to a more conservative capital structure and lower refinancing risk, which to us supports a modest valuation premium.

Our view is that while CAR is marginally safer than its two main peers, that added safety isn't enough to warrant a meaningful premium. In a market downturn, all three would likely face similar pressures, and the slight differences in leverage or interest coverage wouldn't significantly change the outcome.

Superior Asset Quality for Long-Term Investors

We think CAR's debt levels reflect a defensive posture, and their capital recycling strategy supports that positioning.

In Q1 2025, CAR sold nearly $400 million worth of assets and redeployed $137 million into three newly built properties. Management also highlighted that 16% of the total portfolio is now classified as 'recently constructed,' signalling a clear effort to improve asset quality. An additional $42.5 million was invested in capital upgrades such as suite improvements, common areas, and building systems, further supporting long-term value protection.

We think the high-quality assets in CAR's portfolio are reflected in its high occupancy rates. In Ontario, CAR's properties are 99% occupied, while in Calgary, occupancy is at 97.9%. The REIT has been able to raise rents while also maintaining strong occupancy. We view this as a clear indicator of the portfolio's overall strength and appeal to tenants.

Risks

We've identified three key risks that we believe are material to the REIT's performance and our thesis. However, it's important to note that these risks are not exhaustive, and other factors, including the macroeconomic environment, should also be considered before making any investment decisions.

The primary risks to our thesis are a) changes to domestic immigration targets, which could either fuel growth or prevent the stabilization of average monthly rent growth, b) a potential liquidity crunch during a market recession, which could leave CAR struggling to find lenders, and c) a domestic recession that leads to consumer weakness and increased credit losses.

Domestic immigration policy has been a significant driver of growth for the REIT, and any shift in policy could impact demand for rental properties. In the event of a market downturn or recession, liquidity could become a major concern for CAR. If credit markets tighten and lenders become more cautious, CAR may face difficulties refinancing existing debt or securing capital for new investments. Additionally, a domestic recession could lead to weakened consumer spending, which would not only affect rent payments but also increase credit losses for the REIT and administrative costs.

Conclusion

For long-term investors, we see CAR as a relatively safe, defensive investment, and it's Canada's largest residential REIT. The REIT's regional diversification is sufficient with 48.5% of revenue coming from Ontario, 17.2% from Quebec, 14.1% from British Columbia, 6.9% from Halifax, and 5.3% from Alberta.

With the recent dividend increase, the forward yield now sits at approximately 3.7%, compared to the four-year average of 3%. Additionally, the FFO dividend payout ratio sitting at 65.4% suggests a reasonable margin of safety to us.

That said, when comparing CAR to other REITs in the same sector, we don't see any glaring advantages that would make it stand out. For this reason, we are recommending a "Hold" rating, as we believe the current valuation reflects a fair balance of risks and rewards.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ALPEK Still Profitable In Worst Part Of The Cycle, Trades At Low Valuation
data.description:
ALPEK (OTCPK:ALPKF) released 1Q25 earnings in late April, after part (but not all) of the tariffs chaos had lessened, and maintained a historically low guidance for FY25.

The company continued to stress that the US polyester, polypropylene, and styrofoam markets remain stressed and over-supplied, with margins that can be compared to the worst of the GFC. Despite this, the company's guidance still points to a level of EBITDA profitability that can cover CAPEX maintenance and growth needs, plus interest payments, and leave a net profit of $200 million.

Trading at about $1.1 billion, the stock offers a 5x earnings multiple on the worst part of the cycle in the US plastics market in decades. With a recovery in margins, Alpek could easily generate half of its market cap in one year of FCF. For this reason, and given the financial sturdiness of the company, I believe Alpek is a Strong Buy.

Challenging Q1 results

The company's Q1 results were the worst in a long time for margins and volumes. The company generated only $125 million in EBITDA, below the quarterly requirement to meet its FY25 guidance (more below).

The main reason was the deterioration of US competitiveness in polyester versus Asian imports because freight costs have receded versus last year. In addition, the company had operational challenges in the plants in Brazil and UK, and import competition in Argentina. Of these, the US factor is the most relevant. Reference margins in both Asia and China (from where Alpek margins are determined) decreased versus Q4.

The company still believes that margins will recover, especially in China, to moderately above current levels because they characterize the current Asian environment as highly stressed, even saying during the call that probably most Chinese producers are generating losses at Q1 margins.

Guidance was maintained despite tariff chaos

The company maintained the FY25 guidance released in 4Q24, which expects EBITDA of $625 million, $150 million in CAPEX ($50 million for maintenance, $100 million for growth), and $200 million in interest. This guidance is based on several assumptions about margins in each market, some of which were negated during Q1, so there is guidance risk.

For example, Asian and Chinese PET margins were expected to average $270 and $160 per ton (FY25 guidance announcement), respectively, but they sat at $278 and $140 for the quarter (1Q25 presentation). As mentioned, management believes the Chinese margins are unsustainable. For polypropylene, the expectation is margins of $0.14/lb, which has so far been proven true. Finally, the company expected containerized freight from Asia to decrease in cost, increasing the competitiveness of Asian imports. This has also proven true (unfortunately) with the China Containerized Freight Index making new lows. We need to wait for the data after the resumption of US-China trade.

Operational resiliency in historic low margins

One of the main positive points in my evaluation of Alpek is that the company is able to operate profitably, covering CAPEX and interest, in one of the worst market cycles for plastics in the US.

The polypropylene $0.14/lb margins were only lower during the GFC ($0.12/lb, mentioned during the call), and adjusted for inflation, they are even lower now. For PET, the Asian margins bottomed at $270 in 2020, and are today hovering barely above that (Institutional Presentation).

In this context, the company can produce at an EBITDA margin of 7.5% (1Q25 results). If the current margins have not been seen in years, it means that many producers in Asia and the US are underwater. Many probably cannot cover interest, not to mention CAPEX. Eventually, they will curtail production, and margins will rise.

Further, the company's end markets are 70% food and beverage and 20% consumer goods, or 90% in defensive industries (Institutional Presentation). Demand for these packages will not decrease significantly during a challenging economic period.

This all offers a level of downward protection for Alpek. Still, if producer margins were to decrease, I believe Alpek could still maintain a good level of profitability, as shown below for gross and EBITDA profits, when comparing with previous challenging margin periods (for example, 2012-2016 from the Institutional Presentation). The current breakeven rate for EBITDA is about $300 million ($200 million in interest plus $100 million in CAPEX), compared to an adjusted and guided level of $600 million.

Valuation is very attractive

If guidance is met, the company will generate $200 million in cash-based income (EBITDA minus CAPEX minus interest and taxes of 25%). Guidance could not be met if margins decrease even more, but I believe that situation is unlikely to remain, given the comparison with previous stressed margin levels in the plastics markets.

Compared to this, Alpek trades at a market cap of $1.1 billion, or barely above 5x FY25 guided earnings. That is, a company that is one of the lowest cost producers in the market (otherwise it would not be profitable under the worst margins in years), and that is financially solvent and has well-covered interest expenses, trades for 5 times the earnings generated in one of the worst markets in recent history.

This provides three potential sources for return. First, if things continue like this, eventually Alpek can pay dividends and materialize the earnings yield for shareholders. Second, Alpek's multiple can increase if the market realizes that the company will be able to survive the current downcycle and generate more earnings in the future. Finally, Alpek's earnings can improve if the plastics market cycle improves. During the pre-pandemic period, with more normal margins, the company consistently generated operating income in the order of $500 million, and even more than $1 billion during peak-cycle earnings in 2021/22.

For those reasons, I believe Alpek remains very undervalued, and increase the company's rating to a Strong Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SPYI May Be The Income ETF That Gets It Right
data.description:
In a recent analysis on S&P 500 Buy Write Index based ETFs, I had found significant shortcomings and recommended looking for ETFs that do not track the Buy Write very well. The Neos S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (BATS:SPYI) could be that index which generates the alpha missing in the vast majority of ETFs based on option writing I have analyzed so far.

I start with my overall stance on option writing strategies as generally underperforming the underlying it works on, over long time frames. And therefore, you will find, in my earlier writings, calculations showing how you can invest directly in the underlying, match payouts periodically, and still end up with better ROI (including payouts). But there are other uses cases where such strategies may still be effective, which we will examine in greater detail in this analysis.

Underperformance Versus the Underlying

I have generally seen ETFs that are more aggressive in their option positioning (closer to the money) do better as they collect enough premium on wins to offset large losses that are bound to happen when positions turn negative. Now, SPYI does not give out exact specifics of its option positioning. One very distant measure of aggressive position is aggressive payouts (though that can be achieved simply through NAV erosion, which is also quite likely, as seen in several of these ETFs). SPYI pays out lesser than say a YieldMax income ETF. Whether that means less aggressive positioning of options and therefore worse performance over time, is what we will also see in this analysis. SPYI could be doing the option positioning right and paying out less aggressively, which is probably a better long term ploy.

Optimum underlying volatility is also a good predictor of options writing based ETF performance, but that works out more for single stock option writing ETFs than index-based ETFs (single stocks have sharper movements than a diversified index/portfolio).

SPYI recreates the S&P 500 index, sometimes with assumptions on material holdings and layers the underlying created with sold S&P 500 call spreads (meaning it will not reduce downside moves of the underlying very well, but will be able to capture some upside in case of rallies). SPYI has opportunity of capturing alpha in the construction of the underlying (cash positions) as well as the more obvious options positions. I am using SPY as a proxy for the underlying index and comparing it with the total return of SPYI (payouts + capital changes).

Overall returns expectedly are lower for SPYI (52% gains in SPY vs 37% gains including payouts for SPYI since inception), but if it shows a less volatile ownership of the underlying, it may still have some merits as an income plan. Remember, option writing strategies are expected to work best in sideways to slightly correcting cycles. The over-performance in these markets should be the reason to prefer the income plan over simply owning the underlying (in this case SPY) and simulating payouts matching the income plan. As seen, since inception, markets have been trending upwards and such markets often skew performance of income plans. Income plans should be all weather and then its underperformance vs. the underlying becomes acceptable.

Zooming in to what I saw as the most significant down market for S&P 500 since SPYI's inception, I see a $10K investment in SPY would have seen a 15.5% erosion compared to SPYI's total return being down by 13%. While this is not a huge lot of protection, it is somewhat reasonable compared to opportunity loss seen in the overall bull market since SPYI inception, which also played out for a longer tenure. A longer sideways market can show commensurate outperformance for SPYI. One of the reasons I had rejected JEPQ is its lack of downside protection, which is not the case for SPYI.

Conclusion - Although long-term investors can replicate payouts from SPY and still beat SPYI, there are some use cases for SPYI. Works well in sideways to downward markets. And in some way, provides a less volatile ownership experience of riding the SPY or S&P 500. This is at reduced management hassles of replicating income payouts from SPY and potentially better tax benefits (although income payouts can be structured to be long term capital gains and can be not much worse off, not significantly).

Income Specifics

SPYI does not payout aggressively. The payout yield of 12.52% will suit those investors who use SPYI to ride SPY with reduced volatility and derive income. Investors who look for higher aggressive payouts to de-risk themselves from the underlying and use the income to invest elsewhere for larger alpha, will not be disappointed, but the pace of returns still is well below some other income ETFs, such as the YieldMax ones. The YieldMax ETFs return 30-40% of the capital as payouts in a year, compared to the ~30% returns in the form of payouts for SPYI. There is a lot to like about the pacing of the payout for SPYI overall. It balances NAV erosion vs significant income generation very well in comparison to alternatives. Below is a chart showing how the NAV erodes faster in another aggressive payout ETF than SPYI.

Investment Advice

SPYI is certainly not a growth vehicle, and that is obviously not its stated objective. Income generation is decent and stable. The underlying NAV erosion is not as great as most income ETFs around. Performance is also acceptable with an income plan's limitations considered.

I would rate SPYI as a "Buy" for the specific use cases of income and riding S&P 500 with reduced volatility. You will stay invested for a longer time (less NAV erosion) and also grow your funds along with periodic income derived.

data.title: Carter's: Low Earnings Multiple Cannot Offset Its Negative Market Trends
data.description:
Carter's (NYSE:CRI) released 1Q25 earnings last month in the middle of the tariff war, and after recently changing its CEO. The company did not provide yearly guidance, but was confident for Q2 (April MTD).

Although most of the supply chain for Carter's had already been derisked, the US-China agreement announcement provides more certainty around the mid-term future of the US economy (avoiding the recession guaranteed by an embargo-type tariffs).

Still, I believe CRI is not an opportunity at current prices despite the improved macro outlook and the low optical multiple. The name trades at a 7.5x trailing earnings level, but has been losing sales and profitability ever since the post-pandemic. Unless this trend is reversed, a 7.5x multiple is not attractive. I prefer to wait and see how the new CEO sails the boat. I maintain my Hold on CRI.

Back to square one

When CRI released earnings for Q1 a month ago, it seemed as if those earnings could not tell anything about the future, because everything was about to change.

Fortunately, after the US-China agreement on tariffs from this weekend, it seems that very little is about to change, and that the results from last year and Q1 are important.

The company's exposure to China is very low anyway (2% according to the Q1 call), with 75% of production coming from Vietnam, Bangladesh, India, and Indonesia. However, the company was exposed to a US recession, a risk that is now moderated.

Management transition brings an outsider

The company's Chairman and CEO (30 years at Carter's and 15 as CEO) retired in March. Interestingly, he was replaced by an outsider (Doug Palladini) instead of by an internal manager. In principle, and without much more to judge the decision on, I would have preferred an insider.

Palladini comes from 3 years in consulting, and before that had been 17 years in Vans (VFC), where he reached brand President. From Doug's experience (Vans, Skullcandy), he seems to be more experienced in a different target customer (teenagers and young adults), but comes from a similarly sized brand.

He made very few comments during the Q1 call, which was handled by Carter's CFO.

Not great results with caveats

Q4 had been a good quarter for Carter's, as the company posted growing sales for the first time since 2021. The company's more aggressive pricing strategy was improving sales growth, albeit also impacting gross and operating margins.

Q1 was not the same, with comparables down 5% in US retail, International, and US wholesale. The company did not put the blame on a specific factor, but cited that Easter came later this year (end of April or Q2 instead of end of March for 2024). The impact was not quantified, but the company did mention that on a two-month basis, March-April was up 4% in comparables. March is 50% of Q1 sales, according to the call, so Easter can have a big impact.

In terms of profitability, the mix of lower gross margins (from more promotional activity) and sales deleverage continues to eat into profits. Operating income was down 37% for the quarter on an adjusted basis (adding back $6 million in CEO transition costs and $3 million in other restructuring).

Valuation and long-term challenges

Although the company did not provide guidance at the time, we can now expect 2025 to be more similar than disruptive compared to 2024. The tariffs are not so high as to generate a significant inflationary pressure on consumers, and both the supply chain and aggregate demand impacts of the chaotic April will eventually return to the mean.

Carter's now trades at a market cap of $1.25 billion, and the company generated $165 million in net income last year, or a 7.5x trailing earnings multiple.

For a company with good brand value, this should be attractive, but Carter's faces significant structural challenges. Without considering 4Q24, the company has been almost four years losing sales, which has translated into lower store profitability and much lower operating margins. This trend remained unabated as of 1Q25.

The company started to lean more heavily into proportionality in the second half of 2024, as a way to compete with more aggressive players in the market. I do not particularly like this strategy because price competition tends to lead to a race to the bottom, as shown for example by another (much more challenged) children's apparel retailer, Children's Place (PLCE).

Because the company had not gone through similar cycles in the past, it is very hard to forecast that margins and sales will return to a previous mean. The situation seems indicative of an underlying competitive trend.

With that in mind, and assuming only a 5% revenue decline, plus a 2pp operating margin decline, we would be talking of $120 million in net income for FY25, or already a more than 10x multiple.

For that reason, I believe the optically low earnings multiple on Carter's is justified. The situation would change with an improvement in sales, but most importantly in profitability. This may take a long time, but we should see some signals of progress in FY25.

Until then, I prefer to wait, and maintain a Hold rating on the name.

For Investor A shares: Total 0.79%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses 0.79%. Institutional and Investor A shares have contractual waivers with an end date of 06/30/2026 terminable upon 90 days' notice. For certain share classes, BlackRock may voluntarily agree to waive certain fees and expenses in which the adviser may discontinue at any time without notice. Expenses stated as of the fund’s most recent prospectus. Data represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to blackrock.com for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 4.25% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.44%; for Investor A shares: 0.69%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 0.29% (Institutional shares) and 0.24% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • Income accrual canceled out negative price action as yields rose across the curve. The fund held a shorter duration position (lower sensitivity to interest rate movements) for most of the quarter. Higher current yield accrual benefits the fund when yield contributes a greater portion of the total return than price appreciation, with the quarter characterized by depreciation.
  • The fund had a quality bias. The cash and cash equivalent reserve was elevated at 11% due to the fund’s exposure to Treasury bills. Duration of six years was half a year below the benchmark, while 16.8% of securities were exposed to alternative minimum tax bonds. Leverage (borrowed capital) from tender option bonds was stable at 0.8%. The fund had overweight allocations to the corporate-backed municipal and transportation sectors.

Contributors

Detractors

The fund’s high income accrual, lower duration exposure, and higher-quality bias contributed to relative returns. Longer maturity securities underperformed shorter maturities as longer-term rates increased and the yield curve steepened. A robust new issue calendar allowed the managers to purchase attractively structured coupons at higher yields. The fund has become less negatively convex (where duration lengthens as rates rise), which should provide better price appreciation potential in future rate declines and caused duration to rise. The elevated cash and cash equivalent reserve was also beneficial.

Longer maturity, higher duration securities decreased in value as longer-term rates rose and the yield curve steepened. The market has become more concerned about longer maturities as the U.S. Treasury will debate how to finance larger deficits and roll over lower-yielding debt. High yield securities were a negative contributor as spreads for illiquid holdings widened, notably in the workforce housing and charter school sectors. Hedging weighed on relative performance, though it did not lead to a negative absolute total return.

Further insight

During the quarter, long-term interest rates rose, concerns about mutual fund cash flows increased, new issuance was robust, and questions were raised about the future tax treatment of the municipal asset class. Californian securities’ credit quality, while generally strong, is being debated at a local level as federal and state grants seem to be less forthcoming to cover budget shortfalls, and demographic trends work against the revenue portion of both state and local budgets. Consequently, Californian municipal bonds are relatively inexpensive at this point, especially for longer maturities. This yield relationship should be watched as the curve adjusts to future debt issuance forecasts.

★★★★★ Morningstar OverallTM
